title: Peritoneal dialysis catheter insertion
executiveSummary: Assist in the insertion of a Tenckhoff catheter using sterile technique, infraumbilical approach, and immediate postoperative care according to ISPD/KDOQI.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Verify patient identification, prescription, informed consent, and fasting status according to the surgical protocol.
2. Confirm administration of prophylactic antibiotics if indicated.
3. Prepare sterile materials: PD catheter (Tenckhoff), sterile drape, gloves, antiseptic, and an occlusive dressing.
4. Perform hand hygiene and don sterile barriers according to the assigned role (circulating or scrub nurse).
5. Position the patient in a supine position; disinfect the abdomen with alcoholic chlorhexidine over a wide area; allow to dry completely.
6. Assist with the insertion: infraumbilical incision, dissection to the peritoneum, catheter introduction, and verification of dialysate fluid flow.
7. Verify that the catheter drains and aspirates correctly; secure with sutures according to technique.
8. Dress the wound with a sterile dressing; leave the catheter oriented caudally and secured without tension.
9. Document the type of catheter, site, operator, time, and flow test.
10. Transfer the patient to the unit with postoperative care instructions: monitor for pain, bleeding, peritonism, and catheter function.
11. Instruct the patient: do not wet the wound according to protocol; avoid initial abdominal strain; administer laxatives if indicated.

## Scientific rationale

### Indications

- Initiation of renal replacement therapy with **peritoneal dialysis (PD)** as a chronic modality or bridge.
- Stage 5 chronic kidney disease when PD is the chosen modality (KDOQI 2019).
- Temporary bridge to hemodialysis in selected situations.

### Catheter type

| Feature | Recommendation |
| --- | --- |
| Model | Tenckhoff with two cuffs (superficial and deep) |
| Exit site | Infraumbilical, lateral to the midline |
| Orientation | Distal toward the pelvis (prevent kinking) |
| Tunnel | Downward trajectory configuration (reduces infection) |

### Evidence and recommendations

> **ISPD Guidelines on Peritoneal Access (2020):** Placement by a trained team is preferred; perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis; avoid catheter exit site placement in skin folds or pressure areas (Brown et al., 2020).

> **KDOQI Vascular Access 2019:** Plan access in advance in advanced CKD; PD is a valid modality when the patient is informed and trained (Lok et al., 2020).

### Nursing role

- Preoperative preparation and safety checklist.
- Assistance within the sterile field and documentation of events.
- Immediate postoperative care and initial education.
- Coordination with nephrology for the first exchange according to protocol.

### Postoperative care (first 48–72 h)

- Monitor vital signs, pain, wound bleeding, and signs of peritonism.
- Maintain a dry dressing; perform wound care according to protocol after the initial period.
- Avoid unnecessary manipulation of the catheter.
- Do not initiate exchanges until a medical order is received and flow is confirmed.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Active bleeding or expanding abdominal hematoma.
- Severe abdominal pain, fever, or abdominal guarding (suspected perforation or peritonitis).
- Absence of flow or fluid leakage around the drain site.
- Erythema or purulent discharge at the catheter exit site.

bibliography.citation: Brown EA, et al. ISPD Guidelines for Peritoneal Access. Perit Dial Int. 2020;40(4):297-310.
Lok CE, Huber TS, Lee T, et al. KDOQI Clinical Practice Guideline for Vascular Access: 2019 Update. Am J Kidney Dis. 2020.
Li PK, et al. ISPD Guidelines for Peritoneal Dialysis. Perit Dial Int. 2022 updates.
SATI — Comité de Nefrología. Recomendaciones en terapia de reemplazo renal.
World Health Organization. Guidelines for surgical site infection prevention. WHO, 2018.
